title: Electronic Hall viscosity: hidden indicator for antiferromagnets
authors: Ding Li, Tao Qin, Jianhui Zhou
abstract: The antiferromagnets with negligible stray fields and ultrafast spin dynamics play a crucial role in the fields of energy-efficient spintronics and topological electronics. However, the detection and control of the underlying nontrivial Berry curvature become extremely limited by the vanishing magnetization and anomalous Hall conductivity. Here, we show the electronic Hall viscosity is closely related to the quadruple Berry curvature of Bloch bands and is bounded by the $d$-orbit factor modulated second moment of the quantum volume. Moreover, we derive the symmetry requirement for nonzero electronic Hall viscosity that could characterize antiferromagnetic ordering even when the linear anomalous Hall response gets forbidden. We further examine our key findings in two archetypal antiferromagnets: $d$-wave altermagnet $\mathrm{RuO}_{2}$, and noncollinear $\mathrm{Mn_{3}Sn}$ through direct first-principle calculations. Thus, our work reveals a new and fundamental quantum geometry quantity of generic antiferromagnets and offers a broadly applicable way to design antiferromagnetic spintronics devices via unconventional Hall viscosity.
summary: This work introduces the Electronic Hall Viscosity (EHV) as a novel, fundamental quantum geometric quantity to probe antiferromagnetism. It shows that EHV is related to the quadrupole Berry curvature and provides symmetry-dictated rules to detect magnetic order even when standard measurements fail. The theory is validated using DFT calculations on materials like RuO2 and Mn3Sn, offering a new design principle for spintronic devices.
main_problem: The paper addresses the challenge of detecting and characterizing the nontrivial Berry curvature in antiferromagnets, which is obscured by vanishing magnetization and anomalous Hall conductivity.
main_result: Electronic Hall Viscosity (EHV) is proposed as a new, fundamental quantum geometry quantity that can characterize antiferromagnetic ordering even when the linear anomalous Hall response is forbidden.
method: The authors derive theoretical relationships between EHV and the quadrupole Berry curvature, establishing symmetry constraints using Magnetic Point Groups and calculating bounds using quantum volume moments.
model_or_system: The study focuses on generic antiferromagnets, exemplified by the d-wave altermagnet RuO2 and the noncollinear antiferromagnet Mn3Sn, analyzed using first-principle calculations.
key_observables: Electronic Hall Viscosity (EHV, $\eta_{\gamma\delta\lambda}$), Berry curvature ($\Omega_{\alpha}$), and the quadrupole moment of the Berry curvature.
important_parameters:
The analysis involves symmetry operations (e.g., $C'_{n
u}$) and is applicable in the long-wavelength limit ($\lim_{\omega	o 0}\lim_{q	o 0}$).
main_assumptions: The derivation relies on classifying constraints using Magnetic Laue Groups (MLGs) and assumes the applicability of the theory to generic antiferromagnets.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the relationship between EHV components and symmetry operations for specific materials like RuO2 and Mn3Sn, showing how different magnetic configurations yield distinct, non-zero EHV tensors.
paper_structure: The paper progresses from identifying the detection limitation in antiferromagnets to deriving the EHV as a diagnostic tool. It then establishes the mathematical relationship between EHV and the Berry curvature's quadrupole moment, derives symmetry constraints based on crystal/magnetic symmetries, and finally validates these findings using DFT calculations on model materials.
why_interesting: While focused on condensed matter magnetism, the rigorous use of Berry curvature, symmetry analysis, and the introduction of a novel transport tensor (EHV) provides a sophisticated theoretical framework for characterizing quantum geometric properties in ordered magnetic systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. Schematic of the electronic Hall viscosity ηxyz = kxkyΩz(k) for systems with C4 symmetry on the kx-ky plane. The d-orbital function kxky is plotted on the plane. The colored peaks denote the Berry curvature Ωz(k) with the up/down arrow for the positive/negative sign, implying zero anomalous Hall conductivity.
Figure 2. Two different magnetic configurations of the al- termagnet RuO2 with Néel vector L along (a) a-axis and (c) c-axis. (b) and (d) The corresponding EHV tensor versus the Fermi level.

Figure 3. Magnetic structure of the noncollinear antifer- romagnets Mn3Sn and the calculated EHV tensor. (a) the type-III configuration of Mn3Sn and (b) selected nonzero com- ponents of the EHV. (c) the type-IV configuration of Mn3Sn and (d) selected nonzero components of the EHV.
